query:
Research the company "hormelfoods".

Research the company's recent financial performance and strategic situation: (1) Latest earnings results - specifically any revenue misses, P&L gaps, or margin pressures in the last 2 quarters, (2) Recent CEO changes or new C-suite appointments, (3) Updated or pulled guidance for upcoming fiscal year, (4) Recent cost-cutting initiatives, restructuring, or strategic reviews announced, (5) Production/manufacturing growth rates or capacity challenges, (6) New product launches, platform expansions, or market entries, (7) Company size/revenue scale, (8) Industry vertical and go-to-market complexity (multi-channel, pricing-dependent, production-constrained), (9) Cross-functional operational challenges (media/marketing effectiveness, pricing power, distribution expansion, production capacity interactions).

Focus on the last 6 months. Return a comprehensive summary with specific facts, numbers, and dates.
answer:
{"executive_summary": "- **Margin Inflection Signal**: Adjusted operating margin expanded 80 basis points to 9.9% in Q2 FY2026, up from compressed levels, driven by T&M cost savings and pricing - but GAAP margin remains depressed at 7.3% due to the $61M turkey divestiture loss, signaling the turnaround is real but fragile ([executive_summary[0]] [9]).
- **Leadership Vacuum Risk**: Interim CEO Jeff Ettinger's 15-month term ends October 2026 with no permanent successor named; President John Ghingo oversees all three business segments, creating concentration risk if the CEO search stalls ([executive_summary[1]] [3]).
- **Pricing Power Ceiling**: Turkey costs surged over 25% YoY, beef at record highs, freight up 10-15%; Hormel can pass through some costs in Retail but faces limited ability in Foodservice, where adjusted segment profit fell 13% in Q4 FY2025 ([executive_summary[2]] [13], [executive_summary[3]] [38]).
- **Restructuring Down Payment**: 250 corporate/sales positions cut (9% of that workforce), freeing up to $75M for reinvestment in advertising and growth, with $20-25M in restructuring charges ([executive_summary[4]] [12]).
- **Portfolio Shaping Acceleration**: Divestiture of whole-bird turkey business to Life-Science Innovations and exit from Justin's LLC signal a strategic pivot away from commodity-exposed, low-margin categories toward branded, higher-margin platforms ([executive_summary[5]] [17]).
- **Marketing Capability Gap**: New enterprise-wide CMO Jason Levine (appointed December 18, 2025) fills a role that did not previously exist, underscoring that marketing effectiveness was historically fragmented across segments; Q2 FY2026 advertising spend fell to $34M from $36M prior year ([executive_summary[6]] [44], [executive_summary[0]] [9]).
- **Volume Decline Offset by Price**: Organic volume fell 0.8% in Q2 FY2026 and 6% in Q1 Retail, meaning top-line growth is almost entirely price-driven - a strategy with diminishing returns if consumer elasticity accelerates ([executive_summary[0]] [9], [executive_summary[7]] [7]).
- **Supply Chain Modernization on Track**: The Transform and Modernize initiative executed roughly 90 projects in Q3 FY2025 alone, targeting $250M in annualized operating income growth over its three-year span, with $100-150M in incremental benefits realized by end of FY2025 ([executive_summary[8]] [31]).

---", "q1_q2_fy2026_earnings_top_line_growth_margin_recovery_underway": "Hormel Foods reported its fifth and sixth consecutive quarters of organic net sales growth in Q1 and Q2 FY2026, but the composition of that growth reveals significant tension between price and volume.

**Q1 FY2026** (quarter ended January 25, 2026): Net sales of **$3.03 billion** with organic net sales growth of **2%**. GAAP diluted EPS was **$0.33**, adjusted diluted EPS **$0.34**. GAAP operating margin came in at **8.0%** (up from 7.6% prior year), while adjusted operating margin was **8.2%**, a contraction from **8.5%** in the prior year period, reflecting the ongoing drag from commodity inflation and elevated freight costs. The Retail segment was particularly weak: net sales declined **2%**, volume fell **6%**, and segment profit dropped **19%**, driven by the strategic exit from non-core private label snack nut items and declines in branded and private label packaged deli. Foodservice delivered net sales growth of **7%** with segment profit up **13%**, marking its 10th consecutive quarter of organic net sales growth. International posted net sales growth of **8%** and segment profit growth of **10%** ([q1_q2_fy2026_earnings_top_line_growth_margin_recovery_underway[0]] [7]).

**Q2 FY2026** (quarter ended April 26, 2026): Net sales of **$2.97 billion**, up **2.5%** YoY with organic growth of **3%**. GAAP diluted EPS fell to **$0.29** from **$0.33** in the prior year, heavily impacted by a **$61 million loss** on the sale of the whole-bird turkey business. Adjusted diluted EPS grew double-digits to **$0.40** from **$0.35**, the strongest adjusted EPS growth in recent quarters. GAAP operating margin contracted to **7.3%** due to the divestiture loss, but adjusted operating margin expanded **80 basis points to 9.9%**, the clearest signal yet that cost discipline and pricing are gaining traction. Organic volume decreased **0.8%**, with retail volume down approximately **2%** offset by modest gains in Foodservice and International. Gross profit increased to **$518.5 million** from **$484.4 million**. Advertising investments were **$34 million**, down from **$36 million** in the prior year ([q1_q2_fy2026_earnings_top_line_growth_margin_recovery_underway[1]] [9]).

| Metric | Q1 FY2026 | Q2 FY2026 | FY2025 Full Year |
|---|---|---|---|
| Net Sales | $3.03B (+2% organic) | $2.97B (+3% organic) | $12.1B (+2% organic) |
| GAAP EPS | $0.33 | $0.29 | $0.87 |
| Adjusted EPS | $0.34 | $0.40 | $1.37 |
| GAAP Operating Margin | 8.0% | 7.3% | 5.9% |
| Adjusted Operating Margin | 8.2% | 9.9% | 8.4% |
| Organic Volume Change | Decline | -0.8% | Negative |

The margin recovery from FY2025's depressed 5.9% GAAP operating margin to Q2 FY2026's 9.9% adjusted margin demonstrates the T&M initiative is delivering, but GAAP results continue to be distorted by one-time items including the turkey divestiture and restructuring charges.

---", "leadership_overhaul_interim_ceo_new_president_and_c_suite_rebuild": "The leadership transition at Hormel Foods represents the most significant executive reshuffle in the company's recent history, triggered by the departure of long-time CEO Jim Snee after 36 years of service.

**President John Ghingo** was elevated to President effective **July 14, 2025** (announced June 23, 2025), becoming the 11th person to hold that position. Ghingo previously served as Executive Vice President for Retail, leading the company's largest business unit. His background includes roles as president of Applegate Farms, CEO of a better-for-you snacking company, president of plant-based foods at WhiteWave Foods, and over 15 years at Mondel-ez International. Under the new structure, Ghingo directly oversees the Retail, Foodservice, and International business segments, as well as global operations, supply chain, R&D, and corporate strategy. He was also elected to the Hormel Foods Board of Directors ([leadership_overhaul_interim_ceo_new_president_and_c_suite_rebuild[0]] [3]).

**Interim CEO Jeff Ettinger** returned as Interim Chief Executive Officer for a defined **15-month term** commencing July 14, 2025 and scheduled to conclude in **October 2026**. Ettinger previously served as Hormel's 9th President and CEO from 2005 to 2016 and most recently served as interim president of the University of Minnesota and chairman of The Hormel Foundation. He retains oversight of corporate functions including finance, legal, human resources, corporate communications, and ethics and compliance. The board has dissolved its CEO search committee and targets October 2026 for the installation of a permanent CEO ([leadership_overhaul_interim_ceo_new_president_and_c_suite_rebuild[0]] [3]).

**Interim CFO Paul Kuehneman** was appointed interim chief financial officer and controller, effective **October 27, 2025**, reporting to Ettinger. He oversees treasury and internal controls functions ([leadership_overhaul_interim_ceo_new_president_and_c_suite_rebuild[1]] [42]).

**CMO Jason Levine** was appointed to a newly created enterprise-wide Chief Marketing Officer position on **December 18, 2025**, reporting directly to Ghingo. Levine brings over 20 years of CPG marketing experience, including 19 years at Mondel-ez International where he rose to CMO for North America handling brands such as OREO and RITZ. He most recently served as CMO for PIM Brands and held senior marketing roles at Whisps Snacks and Sabra Dipping Company. His mandate encompasses brand strategy, digital capabilities, cross-channel marketing platforms, and the integration of enterprise business analytics (revenue growth management, brand analytics, shopper analytics) with the company's Brand Fuel innovation hub ([leadership_overhaul_interim_ceo_new_president_and_c_suite_rebuild[2]] [44]).

The creation of the CMO role - a position that did not previously exist at the enterprise level - directly addresses the fragmented marketing structure that analysts identified as a weakness. The previous model had marketing decentralized within each segment, leading to inconsistent brand investment and sluggish innovation pace. Ghingo stated the appointment is "an important step in advancing our marketing capabilities and driving growth across all segments of our business."

Additionally, **Kevin Myers** took over as interim group VP of supply chain after **Steve Lykken** departed in April 2025. Myers also serves as SVP of research and development and quality control, adding supply chain to his portfolio on an interim basis ([leadership_overhaul_interim_ceo_new_president_and_c_suite_rebuild[3]] [31]).

---", "fy2026_guidance_reaffirmed_adjusted_targets_gaap_hit_by_turkey_divestiture": "Hormel Foods has maintained a consistent guidance stance through the first half of FY2026, with adjustments only to reflect the impact of the whole-bird turkey divestiture.

**FY2026 guidance** (as of Q2 earnings, May 28, 2026):
- **Net sales**: $12.2 billion to $12.5 billion
- **Adjusted diluted EPS**: $1.43 to $1.51 (reaffirmed)
- **GAAP diluted EPS**: $1.28 to $1.37 (updated downward from previous GAAP range to incorporate the turkey divestiture loss)
- **Long-term growth algorithm**: 2-3% organic net sales growth, 5-7% operating profit growth

The company reaffirmed its full-year adjusted guidance when it reported preliminary Q1 results on **February 17, 2026**, and maintained it through the Q2 report on **May 28, 2026**. MarketBeat noted that the EPS and revenue ranges are roughly in line with or slightly above Wall Street expectations ([fy2026_guidance_reaffirmed_adjusted_targets_gaap_hit_by_turkey_divestiture[0]] [18]).

This guidance represents a meaningful improvement trajectory from FY2025, where GAAP diluted EPS was only **$0.87** and adjusted diluted EPS was **$1.37**. The midpoint of the FY2026 adjusted EPS range ($1.47) implies approximately 7% adjusted EPS growth, consistent with the long-term algorithm. However, the path depends heavily on commodity cost normalization and continued T&M savings realization. Ettinger described the FY2026 outlook as "intentionally ambitious" while expressing confidence that "we are focused on the right initiatives to return Hormel Foods to profitable growth" ([fy2026_guidance_reaffirmed_adjusted_targets_gaap_hit_by_turkey_divestiture[1]] [13], [fy2026_guidance_reaffirmed_adjusted_targets_gaap_hit_by_turkey_divestiture[2]] [17]).

---", "restructuring_and_transform_and_modernize_dual_cost_cutting_engines": "Hormel Foods is pursuing cost discipline through two overlapping initiatives: a corporate restructuring focused on workforce reduction and the multiyear Transform and Modernize (T&M) program targeting supply chain and operational efficiency.

**Corporate Restructuring** (announced **November 4, 2025**): The company implemented a voluntary early retirement program for a portion of its non-plant workforce, closed many open roles, and reduced certain positions across its office-based workforce. These actions resulted in the reduction of approximately **250 corporate and sales positions**, representing **9%** of corporate and sales staff. Restructuring charges are expected in the range of **$20-25 million**, substantially related to one-time pension benefits, cash severance, stock compensation expenses, and employee benefit costs, with most incurred in Q4 FY2025 and Q1 FY2026. The restructuring is designed to free up as much as **$75 million** for reinvestment in advertising, technology, and innovation capabilities ([restructuring_and_transform_and_modernize_dual_cost_cutting_engines[0]] [12], [restructuring_and_transform_and_modernize_dual_cost_cutting_engines[1]] [13]).

**Transform and Modernize Initiative**: Launched at the end of FY2023, this three-year program targets at least **$250 million** in annualized operating income growth. By the end of FY2025, the company had identified over **150 specific projects** and realized **$100-150 million** in incremental benefits. Roughly **90 projects** were executed in Q3 FY2025 alone. The initiative focuses on transforming the supply chain, minimizing portfolio complexity, investing in data and technology, and enhancing people and processes. Hormel expects to incur an additional **$9-12 million** in restructuring charges in FY2026 as streamlining continues ([restructuring_and_transform_and_modernize_dual_cost_cutting_engines[2]] [31], [restructuring_and_transform_and_modernize_dual_cost_cutting_engines[3]] [6]).

The case of the Tucker, Georgia plant illustrates the T&M approach in action. Hormel ended bacon production at the facility because of the age of the equipment and the substantial investment required to align with long-term strategic objectives. Production was moved to other facilities rather than shut down entirely. This selective rationalization - closing legacy operations while preserving output through reallocation - characterizes the program's design philosophy ([restructuring_and_transform_and_modernize_dual_cost_cutting_engines[2]] [31]).

| Initiative | Scope | Savings Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Corporate Restructuring | 250 positions, corporate/sales | Up to $75M freed for reinvestment | Announced Nov 2025, charges in Q4 FY25/Q1 FY26 |
| Transform and Modernize | 150+ projects, supply chain/ops | $250M annualized operating income growth | $100-150M realized by end FY25 |
| Procurement/Productivity Programs | Logistics, direct/indirect supplies | Further savings across categories | Ongoing |

The restructuring is entirely focused on non-plant workforce, with no manufacturing job cuts announced in this specific initiative - though the T&M program handles plant-level optimization separately.

---", "manufacturing_and_supply_chain_optimization_over_expansion": "Hormel Foods' production strategy in the last six months has emphasized rationalization and modernization rather than capacity expansion, consistent with the T&M philosophy of doing more with existing assets.

**Key manufacturing actions**:
- **Tucker, Georgia plant**: Bacon production ended and relocated to other facilities due to equipment age and investment requirements; roughly 90 T&M projects executed in Q3 FY2025
- **Burke Corporation facility (Nevada, Iowa)**: Major expansion announced, supporting growth in pizza toppings and processed meat categories
- **Memphis distribution facility**: Opened to improve inventory flows and increase distribution capacity in the Mid-South region
- **Hormel Production System**: Launched to standardize work across manufacturing facilities, reducing variability and improving throughput
- **Data and Analytics Office**: Formed to support the T&M initiative with better technology and data access for supply chain planning

Capital expenditures were **$82 million** in Q2 FY2026 and **$75 million** in Q2 FY2025, with the largest projects related to capacity expansions for Hormel branded products. Cash flow from operations was **$179 million** in Q2 FY2026. The company ended Q2 with **$827 million** in cash, a **$156 million** increase from year-end, against total debt (including current maturities) of approximately **$2.86 billion** ([manufacturing_and_supply_chain_optimization_over_expansion[0]] [9], [manufacturing_and_supply_chain_optimization_over_expansion[1]] [31]).

The departure of supply chain leader Steve Lykken in April 2025 and his replacement on an interim basis by Kevin Myers (who also runs R&D and quality control) introduces execution risk. Myers's dual mandate could stretch management bandwidth precisely when the T&M initiative requires focused operational leadership. The company has not announced a permanent supply chain chief.

---", "product_innovation_and_portfolio_shaping_exit_commodity_invest_in_brands": "Hormel Foods is simultaneously shedding commodity-exposed businesses and investing in branded innovation, a portfolio reshaping strategy that reduces revenue scale but improves margin quality.

**Divestitures**:
- **Whole-bird turkey business**: Sold to Life-Science Innovations (LSI); the transaction closed by the end of Q2 FY2026 and generated a **$61 million loss** on sale, which depressed GAAP EPS. The company estimates the divestiture will have minimal impact on FY2026 net sales and adjusted EPS guidance. This move exits a commodity-exposed, bird-flu-vulnerable segment that had been a persistent margin drag.
- **Justin's, LLC**: Divested, with a **$0.03 per share gain** reflected in Q1 FY2026 adjusted EPS reconciliation. Hormel subsequently established a partnership with Forward Consumer Partners (announced December 15, 2025) to continue fueling growth for the Justin's brand under new ownership.
- **Non-core private label snack nut items**: Strategic exit, contributing to the 6% Retail volume decline in Q1 FY2026 but improving margin mix.

**New product launches**:
- **SPAM Dog**: Unveiled **May 14, 2026**, a hot dog variant of the iconic SPAM brand positioned for summer cookout occasions. This represents a platform extension of one of Hormel's highest-equity brands into a new consumption format and occasion.
- **Applegate and Hormel Black Label bacon**: Both called out as strong performers in Q2 FY2026 Retail results, benefiting from brand investment and innovation.
- **Jennie-O ground turkey**: Delivered growth in Q1 FY2026 despite overall turkey supply disruptions, demonstrating branded value-added products can offset commodity headwinds.

**International expansion**: The International segment posted net sales of **$186.2 million** (up **4.3%**) and segment profit of **$22.1 million** (up **20.3%**) in Q2 FY2026, driven by SPAM exports and in-country China business growth. The prior Ceratti brand acquisition in Brazil continues to build Hormel's international portfolio ([product_innovation_and_portfolio_shaping_exit_commodity_invest_in_brands[0]] [9]).

The portfolio reshaping follows a clear pattern: exit low-margin, commodity-tied businesses (whole-bird turkey, private label snacks) and invest in high-margin, brand-differentiated platforms (SPAM Dog, Applegate, Jennie-O value-added). The risk is that near-term revenue growth suffers from the exits while the new innovations take time to scale.

---", "company_scale_and_industry_position": "Hormel Foods Corporation is a **Fortune 500 global branded food company** headquartered in **Austin, Minnesota**, with approximately **20,000 employees**. FY2025 full-year net sales totaled **$12.106 billion** (up **1.6%** YoY, organic growth **2%**). GAAP operating income was **$719 million** (operating margin **5.9%**), while adjusted operating income was **$1.019 billion** (adjusted operating margin **8.4%**). GAAP diluted EPS was **$0.87**, adjusted diluted EPS **$1.37**. The company's FY2025 results were depressed by **$234 million in non-cash impairment charges** in Q4 alone, largely tied to a write-down of its minority stake in Indonesia's Garudafood and the retail snack nuts business, resulting in a **$56 million Q4 net loss** ([company_scale_and_industry_position[0]] [6], Star Tribune).

Hormel operates across three segments with distinct channel dynamics:

| Segment | FY2025 Net Sales | FY2025 Segment Profit (Adj.) | Key Characteristics |
|---|---|---|---|
| Retail | $7.455B (+1.1%) | $495M (-24.4% GAAP) | Largest segment; grocery/mass; volume declining; price-driven growth |
| Foodservice | $3.942B (+2.5%) | $555M (-7.0%) | 11 consecutive quarters organic growth; solutions-based; limited pricing power |
| International | $709M (+1.1%) | $83.3M (adj.) | SPAM-led; China growth; Brazil via Ceratti; small but growing |

The company's industry vertical - branded protein and packaged foods - is characterized by **multi-channel go-to-market complexity** (Retail, Foodservice, International each with different customers, pricing dynamics, and competitive sets), **pricing dependence** (commodity cost pass-through is essential but constrained), and **production constraints** (avian flu, equipment age, capacity rationalization). SG&A expenses were **8.2%** of net sales GAAP (**7.8%** adjusted) in FY2025, with advertising investments of **$148 million** for the full year ([company_scale_and_industry_position[0]] [6]).

---", "cross_functional_operational_challenges_the_pricing_volume_marketing_nexus": "The central operational tension at Hormel Foods is the interaction between pricing power, marketing effectiveness, distribution capacity, and production constraints - a nexus that determines whether the turnaround can sustain itself.

**Pricing power is segment-dependent**. In Retail, Hormel can pass through commodity cost increases more effectively because branded products (SPAM, Skippy, Planters) command shelf loyalty and consumer recognition. In Foodservice, the competitive dynamics are more intense: contract-based relationships with restaurant operators and distributors limit the speed and magnitude of price increases. Q4 FY2025 Foodservice adjusted segment profit fell **13%** partly due to this limited cost pass-through. Management has acknowledged that pricing actions are "essential" but their effectiveness is "constrained by intense competitive dynamics" ([cross_functional_operational_challenges_the_pricing_volume_marketing_nexus[0]] [38]).

**Commodity inflation remains the primary headwind**. Turkey prices surged more than **25%** YoY, pork input costs rose by double digits during FY2025, beef costs are at record levels and expected to remain high throughout FY2026, and freight/logistics costs increased approximately **10-15%**. Bird flu compounded turkey supply issues; in Minnesota alone, the virus or culling killed more than **700,000 turkeys** since September 2025. The company expects pork costs to ease in FY2026 while beef costs remain elevated ([cross_functional_operational_challenges_the_pricing_volume_marketing_nexus[1]] [13], [cross_functional_operational_challenges_the_pricing_volume_marketing_nexus[0]] [38]).

**Marketing effectiveness is being retooled**. The creation of the enterprise-wide CMO role and the appointment of Jason Levine signal that the company recognized its previous fragmented marketing structure was insufficient. FY2025 advertising investment of $148M (approximately 1.2% of net sales) is low relative to CPG peers, and Q2 FY2026 advertising actually declined to $34M from $36M. The restructuring aims to free up $75M for reinvestment, but the question is whether increased marketing spend can reverse the volume declines in Retail before price-driven growth reaches its elasticity limit. SWOT analyses have identified "sometimes-sluggish innovation pace" as a weakness exposing the company to agile private labels and shifting consumer diets ([cross_functional_operational_challenges_the_pricing_volume_marketing_nexus[2]] [24]).

**Distribution expansion is underway** but measured. The Memphis distribution facility opening improves inventory flows and distribution capacity in a key geographic region. The Hormel Production System standardization and Data and Analytics Office investments aim to improve supply chain predictability. However, the interim leadership of the supply chain function (Kevin Myers serving dual roles) creates execution risk during a period when the T&M initiative requires focused operational oversight.

**Production capacity interactions** are complex. The company is simultaneously closing legacy production (Georgia bacon line), relocating output to other facilities, expanding Burke Corporation for pizza toppings, and opening new distribution capacity. This portfolio of changes requires careful sequencing: if capacity exits outpace capacity additions, service levels could suffer. The bird flu impact on turkey production adds an external disruption layer that further strains the system.

The core challenge is that volume is declining (organic volume -0.8% in Q2, Retail volume -6% in Q1) while price increases are driving top-line growth. This is a classic **margin-of-safety** concern in the Graham-Dodd tradition: when revenue growth depends on price rather than unit volume, the sustainability of that growth depends on the price elasticity of demand, which steepens as prices rise further. If consumers trade down to private labels or substitute proteins, the volume decline accelerates and the pricing lever loses effectiveness. The new CMO's mandate to strengthen brand equity is therefore not just a growth initiative but a defensive necessity.

---", "synthesis_can_the_turnaround_sustain_through_the_ceo_transition": "Hormel Foods in mid-2026 sits at a genuine inflection point. The adjusted margin expansion in Q2 (9.9% vs. 8.4% FY2025 full year) demonstrates that the T&M initiative and corporate restructuring are delivering tangible cost savings. The portfolio reshaping - exiting commodity turkey and private label snacks, investing in SPAM Dog and Applegate innovation - follows a sound strategic logic of margin quality over revenue scale. Analysts from JPMorgan ("approaching an inflection point"), BNP Paribas ("attractive and prudent" outlook), and Oppenheimer ("much better than feared") express cautious optimism ([synthesis_can_the_turnaround_sustain_through_the_ceo_transition[0]] [13]).

However, three structural tensions could undermine the recovery:

1. **Pricing vs. Volume**: Growth is almost entirely price-driven, with organic volume declining. The economic moat (in the Buffett/Munger framework of durable competitive advantage) that allows pricing power without volume loss is strongest for SPAM and Applegate but weakest for commodity-exposed categories. The new CMO must rebuild brand equity fast enough to prevent elasticity from eroding the price lever.

2. **Interim Leadership Depth**: Ettinger's interim CEO term ends October 2026; Ghingo holds an unusually broad portfolio (all three segments plus operations, supply chain, R&D, and strategy); the supply chain function has interim leadership; and the CFO is also interim. This concentration of "interim" tags across the C-suite creates decision-making risk precisely when the company needs decisive execution on T&M, portfolio reshaping, and marketing investment.

3. **Commodity Dependency**: Even after divesting the whole-bird turkey business, Hormel remains heavily exposed to protein commodity cycles. Record beef costs, volatile pork prices, and avian flu disruptions to turkey supply are external factors that no amount of internal restructuring can fully offset. The company's own guidance concedes beef costs will "remain high throughout fiscal 2026."

The divergence between GAAP and adjusted results also warrants attention: GAAP EPS of $0.29 in Q2 vs. adjusted $0.40, and FY2026 GAAP guidance of $1.28-$1.37 vs. adjusted $1.43-$1.51. The gap reflects real economic costs (divestiture losses, restructuring charges) that adjusted metrics exclude. While the adjustments are standard and disclosed, the pattern of large one-time items (FY2025's $234M impairment, Q2 FY2026's $61M turkey loss) suggests the company's cost structure and asset base are still being rationalized, and further GAAP-disappointing quarters are possible as portfolio reshaping continues.

Ettinger's declaration that "the hiatus is over" on acquisitions signals potential M&A ahead, which could either accelerate the branded portfolio build-out or distract from the operational turnaround that still needs to prove itself. The stock price - down **23%** since the start of 2025 even after a 4% earnings-day pop to **$24.16** - reflects the market's wait-and-see stance on whether this inflection point becomes a sustained trajectory or another false start in what has been, by Ettinger's own admission, "a string of tough years" ([synthesis_can_the_turnaround_sustain_through_the_ceo_transition[0]] [13]).

---"}
